item.title: Washington-backed rare earth group to buy Brazilian miner for $2.8bn
item.description: USA Rare Earth’s deal for Serra Verde highlights America’s push to secure access to critical minerals

item.title: ENRC sues for more than $168mn over botched SFO investigation
item.description: Kazakh miner seeks compensation after UK fraud agency’s probe that closed in 2023

item.title: Miliband unveils move to delink gas and electricity prices
item.description: UK energy secretary’s push comes as Iran war threatens Labour pledge to cut heating bills

item.title: The trade resilience that’s doing Trump a favour
item.description: Absence of freight shipping crisis gives US time to pursue stand-off with Iran

item.title: Netflix’s Reed Hastings: an icon of good leadership and bad governance
item.description: Streamer’s co-founder steps away as his progeny faces its biggest challenge

item.title: Five techy wedding gifts that go beyond the registry
item.description: Fun gadgets for the modern married couple

item.title: UniCredit steps up pursuit of Commerzbank with new plan
item.description: Andrea Orcel says German lender risks becoming ‘increasingly unfit’ for rapidly changing banking market

item.title: William Hill owner in takeover talks with gaming operator Bally’s Intralot
item.description: Evoke in talks to sell itself after taking a hit from UK tax rises on gambling

item.title: Yes, tokens do have real uses in finance
item.description: Tokenisation is taking hold in areas including money, bonds, gold, funds, real estate and private equity

item.title: US stablecoins pose danger to emerging markets, say central bankers
item.description: Growth of dollar-pegged crypto assets under Donald Trump risks accelerating dollarisation and enabling criminal misuse

item.title: Wealth advisers made billions from private capital fees
item.description: Plus, RIP Mark Mobius, BlackRock cools on Europe and Jasper Johns in Bilbao

item.title: Private credit gets the once-over
item.description: Do you want the bad news or the bad news?

item.title: FirstFT: Starmer fights for his future
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: US seizes Iranian ship and Sweden says Russian economy is faltering

item.title: Risk Management: Financial Institutions
item.description: The soaring level of US government debt; how AI is increasing the speed and depth of cyber attacks; and geopolitical shocks have highlighted the need for diversity in cloud providers

item.title: Oil prices set for more turbulence in months ahead, warns Gunvor chief
item.description: World’s fourth-largest independent crude trader says markets may be ‘very choppy’ between April and June

item.title: Russian economy is faltering despite oil windfall, Sweden warns
item.description: Stockholm’s military intelligence head says Moscow is manipulating data to make its economy look better

item.title: FTSE bosses receive 18% pay bump this year in global fight for talent
item.description: Companies are also watering down ESG elements of pay plans as boards reassess whether such awards ‘make sense’

item.title: How the Iran war could hurt Wall Street
item.description: The big unknown is how the conflict could alter the Gulf’s investment priorities in the next year or two

item.title: AI boom poised to be ‘massively disinflationary’, Northern Trust says
item.description: Head of financial services group’s $1.4tn asset management division expects new tech to unleash huge productivity gains

item.title: Germany to begin privatisation of seized Gazprom division
item.description: Head of company now known as Sefe says capital increase could raise up to €2bn

item.title: FTI Consulting to keep Steve Gunby as CEO after dropping succession plan
item.description: Likely replacement quits after board extends longtime leader’s contract to 2029

item.title: The return of the e-merging markets
item.description: For now South Korea and Taiwan are the biggest beneficiaries of the AI wave

item.title: Landlords need to wise up about the Renters’ Rights Act
item.description: Next week, the biggest overhaul of England’s private rented sector in decades comes into effect — yet many have still not engaged with the changes

item.title: Ukraine’s drone pilots hit Russian targets from 500km away
item.description: New internet-based guidance system allows operation of unmanned aerial vehicles far from battlefield

item.title: Kevin Warsh: Trump’s next fall guy at the Fed?
item.description: The nominee to lead the world’s most powerful central bank wants big changes. But there’s risk of confrontation with the president over interest rates

item.title: Life and annuity industry is moving into riskier assets
item.description: Private capital groups have come to control hundreds of billions of dollars of retirement savings

item.title: New technology is increasing the speed and depth of cyber attacks
item.description: Financial services groups are having to adapt rapidly to the changing threat landscape

item.title: Transatlantic divide widens over investment approaches to ESG
item.description: In the US, political pressure has pushed sustainable investing further down the priority list

item.title: Risk management must evolve to remain fit for purpose
item.description: Resilience, crisis preparedness and contingency planning must take centre stage

item.title: Geopolitical shocks highlight the need for diversity in cloud providers
item.description: Some European banks are concerned about their dependence on a handful of US hyperscalers
